scenario:
Situation: Line 3 down at 22:47. Need 500 units of bushing P/N B-447 by 06:00.
Without parts, we miss a $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 tomorrow.

Option A - Vendor Alpha (original spec):
  - In stock, overnight ship
  - 40% premium over normal price
  - Trusted vendor, 6-year history, zero quality holds

Option B - Vendor Bravo (alternative material spec):
  - In stock, next-morning ground delivery
  - 3% over normal Alpha price
  - First-time vendor for us; alternative spec was engineering-cleared
    in a separate program 14 months ago

Engineering is unreachable until 07:00.

decision.rationale: The $42,000 customer commitment ships at 14:00 and Line 3 needs parts by 06:00 — throughput is the dominant priority. Vendor Alpha is in stock, ships overnight, and arrives within the window; the 40% premium is well inside the 60% threshold where I'm authorized to act without escalation. Vendor Bravo is cheaper and has an engineering clearance, but that clearance is from a separate program 14 months ago — my identity rules require 'any engineering clearance' for the alt-spec to be fair game, and technically that clearance exists, but Bravo is a first-time vendor whose blast radius here is the entire $42k commitment on a single production line, which is not a bounded-stakes qualification run. Applying the lesson from the Aurora scramble: when a vetted alternative is in stock and the SLA is at risk, use it — Vendor Alpha is exactly that vetted alternative. I'll document Bravo for a future bounded first-order qualification when the stakes are lower.
identity_clauses_invoked: Premiums up to 60% acceptable when downtime cost > premium delta.
Alt-spec material with ANY engineering clearance is fair game.
First-time vendors are an investment. Use real orders to qualify them when the blast radius is bounded.
Escalation: Cost premium > 60%, OR no engineering clearance of any vintage, OR blast radius covers more than one production line.

2025-08-09 — Aurora capacity scramble: held out for primary supplier; took the 4-hour delay; missed the customer SLA. Lesson: when a vetted alternative is in stock and the SLA is at risk, don't optimize for vendor continuity at the cost of customer commitment.
Throughput / on-time delivery to customer [ranked priority #1]
